comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around a physical implementation of Conway's Game of Life, with commenters debating cost-effective alternatives, the appeal of the project, and the nature of its physicality. Some suggest cheaper hardware options or DIY methods, while others reflect on the artistic or experiential aspects of such installations.

comments.author: kangalioo
comments.text: A much cheaper way might have been to buy a couple of Novation Launchpads. 8x8 full RGB tactile buttons for 90€, MIDI-controllable. Four of those next to another for 16x16 at 360€ plus a little bit for cables and controller comes out at 1/3 the price
comments.analysis: This comment provides a practical and cost-effective alternative to building the project, suggesting commercially available hardware that fits the requirements well.

comments.author: smcameron
comments.text: Something like those switches might be made very cheaply with a 3D printer, possibly a laser cutter, some transparent or semitransparent acrylic sheet, tactile switches and some LEDs. I designed a cheapo replacement for $50 tellite switches and got the price down to about $0.60 Not quite the same, as these are a lot bigger, and getting things down to the desired size might be troublesome. Anyway, here's a little video of my fake cheapo tellite switches: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LaenrgPVCjc
comments.analysis: The comment offers a detailed and feasible DIY approach to reduce costs, supported by personal experience, making it a valuable contribution.

comments.author: eps
comments.text: I saw one in a computer museum in Switzerland. It was a much larger field, it was just large orange LEDs (or were they tubes?), but it also cycled between a dozen of different cell automata games. Something about being able to see individual "pixels" made it really mesmerizing.
comments.analysis: This comment shares a relevant anecdote about a similar installation, adding context and historical perspective to the discussion.

comments.author: self_awareness
comments.text: That's not a "physical" version of game of life -- that's a digital version, like every version, but with bigger pixels.
comments.analysis: The comment critically addresses the definition of 'physical' in this context, highlighting an important conceptual distinction.

comments.author: jFriedensreich
comments.text: how is this real life more than a monitor, its just bigger pixels. i was expecting programmable live cells or something mechanical
comments.analysis: Expresses a common misconception or expectation about physicality, reflecting a desire for a more mechanical or truly tangible implementation.

comments.author: possiblydrunk
comments.text: Nicely done! Scale matters.  If you make something big enough relative to its expected size, it will impress and captivate, even if it's simple. General observation, not that the construction here was by any means simple.
comments.analysis: Offers an insightful observation about the impact of scale on perception and engagement with the project.

comments.author: cjfd
comments.text: When I was a teenager, I read a book about assembly language for the commodore and implemented the game of life in a really simple way. I just used the text screen. To switch on a cell, I would put an asterisk ('*') in it. Then I could run my machine code program and it would evolve according to the rules of the game of life.
comments.analysis: Shares a personal experience related to the game, adding nostalgic value but less directly relevant to the physical implementation discussion.

comments.author: slow_typist
comments.text:
Très cool.

A grid of capacitive touch sensors could be printed directly on the pcb, bringing down costs by a degree of magnitude. Real switches are much more satisfying though.
comments.analysis: Suggests a technical alternative to reduce cost while acknowledging the trade-off in user experience.

comments.author: mastermedo
comments.text: A thousand bucks for 17x17 touchscreen. Add a painting frame, hang it on the wall, and you made yourself amazing art for cheap.
comments.analysis: Focuses on the artistic and aesthetic value of the project rather than technical details or cost efficiency.

comments.author: Cthulhu_
comments.text: I love this and would love to see it on a wall at our office or something like that. Maybe there's smaller/cheaper led/switches that would work in a handheld version.
comments.analysis: Expresses enthusiasm and suggests potential for more compact or affordable versions, contributing positively to the discussion.
